full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My friend, Candace Simpson, wrote the following this past week,<|5.30|> <|5.30|> and I've been eager to share this with you.<|8.84|> <|8.84|> Quote, "I've been working on something about profits<|14.02|> <|14.02|> and what a profit is and what a profit isn't.<|21.02|> <|21.02|> And I'm clear, profits don't just turn phrases cleverly.<|27.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Prophets are so sensitive to the world that it appears that they're telling the future.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> What they're really doing is telling the truth about the present that no one wants to hear."<|16.18|> <|16.18|> End quote.<|18.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.40|> Minister Simpson's observation is a helpful way of framing<|5.94|> <|5.94|> the haunting passage from Amos we just heard.<|10.34|> <|12.92|> "The Holy One showed me a basket of summer fruit<|17.98|> <|17.98|> and said, 'Amos, what do you see?'"<|22.42|> <|23.32|> "A basket of summer fruit," I said.<|26.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What us English speakers miss is that in the original language,<|5.94|> <|5.94|> the term "basket of summer fruit."<|9.24|> <|9.24|> This is fascinating, actually.<|11.68|> <|11.68|> And I'm not really...<|14.08|> <|14.08|> In seminary, I will not tell you the grade that I got in Greek.<|17.82|> <|17.82|> I will tell you that I have found the biblical languages<|22.26|> <|22.26|> to be much more interesting on this side of ordination<|26.10|> <|26.10|> than the other side.<|28.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But I did notice this, and it truly is fascinating.<|4.88|> <|5.04|> In Hebrew, the term basket of summer fruit is a pun on the word end.<|15.72|> <|15.72|> End.<|16.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And so when the Holy One, the Creator, shows Amos a basket of summer fruit, Amos is being<|7.60|> <|7.60|> shown the end, which makes so much sense of the dimness of the rest of that passage.<|16.82|> <|16.82|> The end has come upon my people Israel, the Holy One says to Amos, and your songs will<|22.44|> <|22.44|> transform into welling.<|25.16|> <|25.16|> Dead bodies will be everywhere.<|28.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.60|> This is not uplifting material.<|5.64|> <|7.88|> I've often said that prophets do not make great guests at cocktail parties,<|14.00|> <|15.24|> because who can enjoy a cocktail party when someone is foretelling gloom?<|20.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But as Candace, my friend, said earlier, prophets are so sensitive to the present that we assume<|10.28|> <|10.28|> that they are seeing the future.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> Amos, in the style of prophets before and since, gets straight to the heart of his people's<|21.08|> <|21.08|> issue.<|22.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> The ruling economic and political class is enjoying relative prosperity<|7.20|> <|7.20|> at the expense of impoverished people,<|10.40|> <|10.40|> and if the nation does not correct its course,<|14.70|> <|15.90|> there will be harsh consequences for everyone.<|20.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.32|> for everyone.<|2.32|> <|3.98|> Amos here even goes so far as to use apocalyptic hyperbole.<|8.98|> <|10.72|> And apocalypse, our word in English,<|15.40|> <|15.40|> comes from the Greek word that means unveiling.<|19.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So, we use apocalypse sort of in scary ways, but really it just means an unveiling, tearing<|9.62|> <|9.62|> away the veil so we can see things as they actually are.<|15.24|> <|15.24|> And here he uses apocalyptic hyperbole to illustrate the god of justice's disdain<|22.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> for exploitative economic and political arrangements.<|4.90|> <|4.90|> And this is usually what prophets are talking about<|8.20|> <|8.20|> in the Hebrew Bible and in the New Testament.<|11.62|> <|11.62|> I don't know how many of you are familiar with<|15.22|> <|15.22|> the Hebrew Bible scholar Walter Brueggemann.<|18.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But Walter Brueggemann speaks very often of kind of the sociopolitical location of prophets<|11.04|> <|11.04|> in the Hebrew Bible.<|13.02|> <|13.02|> And they were often not just one person.<|16.40|> <|16.40|> So when we see in the Bible a prophet personified as a single individual.<|24.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It's usually a personification of a band or group of people who would travel the Judean countryside and the Israelites' countryside singing doom and gloom.<|15.36|> <|16.40|> So prophets were always poets and musicians as well.<|20.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We can, in our contemporary setting, think of prophets as comedians.<|6.00|> <|6.00|> Comedians often have very searing social commentary sort of cloaked in humor.<|17.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And prophets played that role in ancient Israel.<|3.84|> <|5.12|> So they were kind of these agrarian musicians and poets who would travel the countryside singing and warning the people of God about the course that they were going on.<|20.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.28|> And so he uses this apocalyptic hyperbole<|5.28|> <|5.28|> to illustrate God's disdain<|10.68|> <|11.72|> for exploitative economic and political arrangements.<|16.16|> <|16.16|> And he says this:<|18.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.04|> I will make the sun go down at noon,<|4.08|> <|5.20|> and I will darken the earth in broad daylight.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> I will turn your feasts into sad affairs<|12.64|> <|12.64|> and all your singing into a funeral song.<|16.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.54|> But is this truly hyperbole?<|4.60|> <|6.10|> The more we learn about our climate crisis<|10.72|> <|11.62|> and the harmful effects of unregulated economic and ecological exploitation<|18.08|> <|18.08|> on not only our bodies,<|21.12|> <|22.02|> but on this land,<|24.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> on our ozone,<|3.00|> <|3.00|> on our oceans, on our mountains,<|7.00|> <|7.00|> the clearer it becomes that while profits may not make great cocktail partners,<|14.00|> <|14.00|> they are attuned to things we often refuse to see.<|20.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.44|> And it is then that we are invited to lean into the discomfort<|7.00|> <|7.00|> of hearing difficult things,<|10.14|> <|11.74|> of celebrating a prophet's discipline,<|16.06|> <|16.06|> of waiting for the grace to reorient our lives.<|20.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That's all that repentance is, is a reorientation, a changing of direction of<|8.50|> <|8.50|> one's life, of our common life, of seeking to live gently with one another<|14.50|> <|14.50|> and with the earth. And as one Eucharistic prayer in the Episcopal<|20.90|> <|20.90|> tradition says, "This fragile earth, our island home."<|26.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> we only get one of these,<|3.50|> <|3.50|> and we must be gentle with it as we are gentle with one another.<|9.24|> <|9.24|> Of sitting at Jesus' feet because we've never met anyone quite like him.<|17.76|> <|17.76|> I tend to try to be a little gracious with Martha.<|22.76|> <|22.76|> She always gets kind of the short end of the stick when we hear this story.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But I think Martha and Mary both function in a way that any community needs.<|11.88|> <|11.88|> We need people who are going to sit and pray and contemplate at the feet of Jesus and people<|19.90|> <|19.90|> who are going to keep the train moving.<|23.28|> <|23.28|> We can't have one of either.<|26.12|> <|26.12|> We need both in a community.<|28.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> of practice.<|2.00|> <|3.56|> But there at Jesus' feet<|6.84|> <|8.04|> is where the famine that Amos warned us about ends, isn't it?<|13.84|> <|15.08|> That he promises that, as Richard said this morning,<|19.72|> <|19.72|> when we stop feeling,<|22.36|> <|22.36|> when we stop practicing empathy,<|26.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> we are absent the word of the Lord.<|3.42|> <|6.60|> But when we sit at Jesus' feet,<|9.44|> <|10.72|> when we listen not just to Jesus' words,<|14.26|> <|14.62|> but when we listen even to Jesus' face,<|17.86|> <|19.46|> when we take those nonverbal cues from Jesus,<|23.00|> <|24.20|> we are absent the word of the Lord.<|24.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.24|> We are nourished, we are fed, and we can't help but bring other people to those same feet.<|13.28|> <|13.28|> Because we've never met anyone quite like Him before.<|19.06|> <|19.06|> And we are afraid, and rightfully so, that we won't meet anyone like Jesus again.<|26.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and we come to those feet,<|5.36|> <|5.66|> and we hold back the time when words of warning are finally desired,<|15.08|> <|15.08|> but are nowhere to be found. Amen.<|21.34|>
